expectations.text: fix.md contains a rewritten description that includes at least TWO specific trigger keywords from the user's stated scope (any two of: 'OpenAPI', 'function signatures', 'parameter tables', 'type signatures', 'API reference') AND an explicit 'NOT for' clause naming at least TWO excluded categories from the user's list (any two of: blog posts, marketing copy, README, README files, general writing).
expectations.evidence: The rewritten YAML description includes: 'OpenAPI/Swagger specs, function signatures, parameter tables, type signatures' — all four trigger keywords present. The description also contains: 'NOT for prose writing, blog posts, marketing copy, README files, changelogs, tutorials' — explicitly names blog posts, marketing copy, and README files (three of the five required exclusion categories).

expectations.text: fix.md recommends a concrete activation-testing approach, satisfied by EITHER (a) naming a skill-foundry script by filename — one of `test_activation.py`, `quick_validate.py`, `validate_skill.py`, `analyze_skill.py` — OR (b) listing at least 3 positive trigger prompts AND at least 2 negative trigger prompts to test against.
expectations.evidence: fix.md includes a 'Testing Protocol' section with a 'True-Positive Battery' of 6 prompts (TP1–TP6, e.g. 'Generate an OpenAPI 3.1 spec for my user authentication endpoints.') and a 'False-Positive Guard Battery' of 8 prompts (FP1–FP8, e.g. 'Write a blog post about why we migrated from REST to GraphQL.'). Satisfies criterion (b): ≥3 positive and ≥2 negative prompts.

expectations.text: fix.md uses at least one skill-foundry concept by name — one of: 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth', 'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'.
expectations.evidence: The full text of fix.md was retrieved. None of the required concept names appear: 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth', 'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'. The document explains description improvement techniques in its own language ('domain vocabulary', 'scope anchor', 'explicit exclusions') without using any skill-foundry terminology.

claims.evidence: Write tool call confirmed at /private/tmp/pilot-bench-bc4rpp9h/with/fix.md with full content; output.txt also states 'Saved to `fix.md`'

claims.claim: The rewrite uses domain-specific vocabulary that prevents false positives
claims.evidence: Description anchors on 'machine-readable API reference artifacts' with specific terms: OpenAPI, Swagger, JSON Schema, parameter table, type signature. The NOT-for clause explicitly names blog posts, marketing copy, README files, tutorials, changelogs.

claims.claim: 6 true-positive prompts and 8 false-positive guard prompts are included
claims.evidence: Counted TP1–TP6 (6 entries) and FP1–FP8 (8 entries) in the Testing Protocol section of fix.md.

suggestions.assertion: fix.md uses at least one skill-foundry concept by name — one of: 'progressive disclosure', 'shibboleth', 'shibboleths', 'philosophy-first', 'description engineering', or 'Description Field Engineering'.
suggestions.reason: This assertion is a strong discriminator — the executor produced genuinely high-quality output (correct rewrite, thorough testing protocol) but failed because it didn't use skill-foundry terminology. This is exactly the right kind of assertion for testing whether the skill transmitted its conceptual vocabulary, not just its output pattern.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-calibrated. Expectation 3 (concept names) is the sharpest discriminator — a with_skill run that doesn't use skill-foundry vocabulary suggests the skill's philosophy section isn't being activated. No weak assertions detected.
